title: Por que a democracia não funciona para todos: estatuto social e apoio à democracia na Europa
quantitative_analysis_evidence_quote: All models are linear, hierarchical, mixed-effects models, with respondents nested in countries.
statistical_inference_quote: there are indeed significant differences between country groups in the strength of the effect of status on expectations for liberal democracy
causal_or_explanatory_claim_quote: I assume that the perceived social position of citizens influences both their expectations and evaluations of democracy.
causal_design_quote: All models are linear, hierarchical, mixed-effects models, with respondents nested in countries.
main_variables_or_relationship: The article analyzes how perceived social status, income, education, and occupation relate to democratic expectations and evaluations across liberal, social, and direct democratic dimensions, including variation across post-communist, Eurozone crisis, and Western European country groups.
sample_or_data_source: European Social Survey Round 6 individual-level data for 26 countries, combined with country-level data from the Democracy Barometer and the Standardized World Income Inequality Database.
brief_justification: The full body shows an empirical quantitative article using ESS survey data, EFA, and hierarchical mixed-effects models to test explanatory hypotheses about social status and democratic support. It has statistical inference and causal/explanatory language, but no explicit credibility-revolution identification design; the causal method is best characterized as observational regression with causal claim and no design.
